brief.oneLiner: Build your best streaks without compromising your data privacy.

deliverable.text: Market research: privacy-first habit trackers resonate with indie developers (25-40). Top pains: guilt-driven streak loss, data exfiltration by SaaS vendors, tool sprawl across paid tiers. Competitive whitespace: incumbents (Habitica, Streaks) skew gamified and cloud-dependent. Positioning opportunity = calm, local-first, no mandatory cloud, developer CLI. Willingness to pay: one-time purchase preferred; OSS with optional self-hosted sync resonates strongly.

verdict.reason: The research deliverable is highly on-brief, accurately identifying the target audience (indie developers 25-40), their core pain points (data exfiltration, tool sprawl), and the competitive whitespace (cloud-dependent gamified apps). It clearly aligns with the product features (local-first, zero tracking, CLI) and provides actionable insights on positioning and willingness to pay. It is concise, technical, and perfectly usable for the next stages of the launch.

kit.landingCopy:
Headline: Streaky — habit tracking, local-first.
Subhead: Privacy-first habit tracker built for indie developers. Your data stays on your machine. No account. No cloud dependency. No subscription.
Features: Local SQLite store · AES-256 encrypted export · CLI + lightweight web UI · Zero telemetry · Self-hostable sync.
CTA: Install now — npm install -g streaky — MIT licensed, open source.
tweetThread: Indie devs: your habit tracker shouldn't require an account or exfiltrate your data to the cloud. Meet Streaky—local-first, MIT-licensed habit tracking. No accounts, no telemetry, no gamified bloat. Just your streaks. 🧵
1/ 100% local data storage via SQLite. Your behavioral data never leaves your machine unless you explicitly export it (AES-256 encrypted, naturally). No vendor lock-in, no cloud dependency.
2/ Built for the terminal-first workflow. Keyboard-driven navigation, a minimalist web UI for when you need it, and a robust CLI to mark off habits without breaking your coding flow.
3/ Zero third-party tracking or analytics. We don't know what you're doing, and we don't want to. Build your best streaks without compromising your data privacy.

4/ Stop paying monthly subscriptions for cloud-dependent habit SaaS. Streaky is open source and ready to go. 

Install now: npm install -g streaky
kit.shortPitch: Streaky: local-first, MIT-licensed habit tracking for devs. No cloud, no accounts, zero telemetry. Just SQLite and your streaks.
kit.phHnBlurb: Hey HN, I built Streaky because existing habit trackers are either cloud-dependent SaaS traps or too gamified for my workflow. Streaky is a privacy-first habit tracker built for indie developers. It uses a 100% local SQLite database, features zero third-party tracking or analytics, and requires no accounts. You interact with it via a keyboard-first CLI and a minimalist, distraction-free local web UI. If you want to sync across devices, you can self-host the sync server. It's MIT licensed and installed via npm. Build your streaks without selling your behavioral data.
kit.readmePolish:
# Streaky — habit tracking, local-first.

Privacy-first habit tracker built for indie developers. Your data stays on your machine. No account. No cloud dependency. No subscription.

## Features
- **100% Local Storage:** Your data lives in a local SQLite store. No cloud lock-in.
- **Zero Telemetry:** No third-party tracking or analytics. Period.
- **Keyboard-First Navigation:** Built for the terminal. Mark off habits via CLI or a minimalist, distraction-free web UI.
- **Secure Exports:** AES-256 encrypted exports for when you need to move your data.
- **Self-Hostable Sync:** Optional, self-hosted sync for multi-device workflows.

## Quick Start
Install globally via npm:
```bash
npm install -g streaky
```
*MIT licensed, open source.*

requirements.brief: Research the market for a privacy-first habit tracker app targeting indie developers. Key areas: 1) Competitive landscape — existing habit trackers (Streaks, Habitica, Loop, etc.), their privacy posture, and gaps. 2) Indie developer audience profile — what privacy concerns they have, what tools they already use, what messaging resonates. 3) Positioning recommendations — how to differentiate a privacy-first habit tracker (no cloud sync by default, local-first storage, open-source, encryption). 4) Key messaging angles for landing page copy. 5) Pricing/monetization insights. Deliver a concise research brief (1-2 pages) that the landing copy writer can directly use.

Competitors (Habitica, Streaks) are cloud-dependent and gamified — we are calm, private, and developer-respecting. Monetization: one-time purchase / OSS with optional self-host. Keep it concise, developer-native, and skimmable.
